coredata.dc:title: Integration of carbon dots and polyaniline with TiO2 nanoparticles: Substantially enhanced photocatalytic activity to removal various pollutants under visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Feizpoor, Solmaz
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Yubuta, Kunio
coredata.dc:description: In this study, ternary photocatalysts were fabricated through integration of carbon dots (CDs) and polyaniline (PANI) with TiO2 nanoparticles by a facile route. The photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, HRTEM, EDX, BET, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, and PL instruments. Among the photocatalysts, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite exhibited much higher photocatalytic performance for removal of RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine in comparison with the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, and TiO2/PANI (20%) photocatalysts under visible light. The degradation rate constant of RhB by TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite was 481 × 10−4 min−1, which is almost 36.5 and 2.6 times more than those of the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs photocatalysts, respectively. It was confirmed that O2 − played predominant role in RhB degradation. Finally, the mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity was also discussed. The improved visible-light photocatalytic performance was related to substantial absorption of visible light, diminished recombination of the charge carriers, and enhanced specific surface area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/CDs/PANI
TiO2/CDs
Visible-light photocatalyst
Water pollutants

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CDs and TiO₂/CDs nanocomposites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support ofthis work by University of Mohaghegh Ardabili-Iran is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation procedure of the TiO₂/CDs/PANI nanocomposites.
XRD patterns for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs samples, and TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different weight percentages of PANI.
(a) EDX spectra for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), CDs, and TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different weight percentages of PANI. (b–f) EDX mapping for the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM and (b) HRTEM images of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite: (a) Survey scan and high-resolution spectra for: (b) Ti 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) C 1s.

(a) FT-IR spectra for the TiO₂, TiO₂/CDs (0.5 mL), CDs, and TiO₂/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different weight percentages of PANI. (b) UV–vis DR spectra for the TiO₂, TiO₂/CDs samples, CDs, and TiO₂/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different weight percentages of PANI. (c) Plots of (αhν)2 versus hν for the samples.

(a) Photodegradation of RhB over the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, TiO2/PANI (20%), and TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different weight percentages of PANI. UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation over the (b) TiO2, (c) TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and (d) TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, TiO2/PANI (20%), and TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites with different compositions. (b) PL spectra for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposites. (c) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples.
Plausible mechanism for the increased photocatalytic activity of TiO₂/CDs/PANI nanocomposites.
The degradation rate constants of RhB over the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite in presence of various scavengers.
(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite prepared at different times. (b) PL spectra for the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite prepared at different times.
The degradation rate constants of RhB with different concentrations over the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite.
Theoretical and experimental weight percentages for the elements in the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposites.
Comparison between photocatalytic performance of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite with the literature.
Textural properties of the TiO₂, TiO₂/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO₂/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalysts.
Rate constants for photodegradations of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine and the linear regression coefficients from a plot of ln(C0/C)= kt.

head.ce:title: Integration of carbon dots and polyaniline with TiO2 nanoparticles: Substantially enhanced photocatalytic activity to removal various pollutants under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University, 2-1-1 Katahira, Aoba-ku, Sendai 980-8577, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, ternary photocatalysts were fabricated through integration of carbon dots (CDs) and polyaniline (PANI) with TiO2 nanoparticles by a facile route. The photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, SEM, HRTEM, EDX, BET, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, and PL instruments. Among the photocatalysts, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite exhibited much higher photocatalytic performance for removal of RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine in comparison with the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, and TiO2/PANI (20%) photocatalysts under visible light. The degradation rate constant of RhB by TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite was 481 × 10−4 min−1, which is almost 36.5 and 2.6 times more than those of the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs photocatalysts, respectively. It was confirmed that O2 − played predominant role in RhB degradation. Finally, the mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity was also discussed. The improved visible-light photocatalytic performance was related to substantial absorption of visible light, diminished recombination of the charge carriers, and enhanced specific surface area.

ce:section.ce:para: The huge development of industry and growth of population have produced environmental crisis in the earth [1,2]. As well known, heterogeneous photocatalysis has promising ability to address energy shortage and environmental contamination crises using the sun light [3–6]. Because of appealing properties including cheap price, excellent chemical and thermal stability, and green benign, semiconductor-based photocatalytic processes using TiO2 have attracted remarkable attentions of researchers worldwide [7–9]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic processes using TiO2 are far from large scale utilization, owing to its wide band gap and fast recombination of the photoinduced e−/h+ pairs [10,11]. To overcome these shortcomings, different strategies including metal/nonmetal doping and integration with small band gap semiconductors are employed to improve photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [12–25].

Polyaniline (PANI), as a polymeric narrow band gap semiconductor, has attracted much more attention in the field of photocatalysis, owing to considerable mobility of charge carrier, facile synthesis process, environmentally benign, and unique optical properties [26]. This semiconductor, with band gap energy of about 2.8 eV, has been applied as visible-light sensitizer in photocatalytic processes [27–32]. Fortunately, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PANI is more negative relative to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons are simply transfer from the LUMO of PANI to the CB of TiO2, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance [30]. To more pronounced transfer of charge carriers between two semiconductors, carbon dots (CDs) are used, as low-price mediator [33].

Herein, we employed a facile approach to synthesize ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites. The fabricated photocatalysts were applied for visible-light photocatalytic degradations of RhB, MB, and fuchsine (as cationic dyes) and MO (as anionic dye) pollutants. Investigation of the photocatalytic activities under visible light exhibited substantially enhanced activity for the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites relative to the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, and TiO2/PANI (20%) photocatalysts. Additionally, an electron transfer mechanism was also proposed for the highly improved performance of the ternary nanocomposites.

A Philips Xpert diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) was employed to record XRD patterns for analyzing the phase structures. The HRTEM image was provided using an EM-002B (TOPCON) microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. An SEM with model of LEO 1430VP was employed to study surface morphology and quantitative elemental analysis using EDX spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra were provided by a Perkin Elmer Spectrum RX I apparatus. The UV–vis diﬀ ;use reﬂectance spectra (UV–vis DR spectra) were measured by a Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer. Chemical states of the ternary photocatalyst were analyzed by XPS (JPS-9010MC, JEOL) using non-monochromated Mg Kα radiation. The BET surface areas of the samples were estimated with a Belsorp Mini II analyzer. A PerkinElmer (LS 55) fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to provide PL spectra. Finally, a Bandelin ultrasound processor of model HD 3100 (75 W, 20 kHz) was used to provide ultrasound radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs and TiO₂/CDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 3.0 g citric acid (Merck, 99%) and 3.0 g urea (Merck, 99%) were mixed with 10 mL of water. Thereafter, the solution was heated for 5 min in a domestic 750 W microwave oven. During the heating process, the solution changed from a colorless to a light brown solution and finally to a dark brown solid. Then, the formed solid was dissolved in water and centrifuged for 30 min with 8000 rpm to remove the agglomerated particles [34].

For synthesis of the TiO₂/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, in which 0.5 mL is volume of the applied CDs, 0.4 g TiO2 (P25, Degussa, 99.5%) and 0.5 mL of the CDs solution were dispersed in 150 mL water by ultrasonic irradiation for 10 min. Thereafter, the suspension was dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The other TiO₂/CDs nanocomposites were fabricated with similar route by changing the applied volume of the CDs as 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mL.

The aniline monomer was oxidized by ammonium persulfate in the presence of TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite. In situ polymerization processes were conducted at a constant mole ratio of aniline to ammonium persulfate 1:3. The amounts of aniline were predetermined to obtain ternary nanocomposites with 10%, 20%, 30%, and 40% of PANI. In the case of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite, the weight percentage of the oxidized aniline vs. TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) was 20%. Also, other photocatalysts were synthesized in the same manner as the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite. For preparation of 0.5 g TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite, 0.4 g of the TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite was suspended in 150 mL water by sonication for 10 min. Afterwards, 0.098 mL aniline (Loba Chemie, 99%) was added to the suspension and stirred for 30 min. Then, 0.073 g ammonium persulfate (Loba Chemie, 98%) dissolved in 20 mL water and it was dropwise added to the suspension and stirred for 240 min. The resulted dark green product was washed with water and ethanol and dried overnight at 60 °C. The fabrication process of the ternary nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Activity of the prepared photocatalysts was tested by decomposing RhB (1.0 × 10−5 M), MO (1.0 × 10−5 M), MB (1.0 × 10−5 M), and fuchsine (0.77 × 10−5 M). The photocatalytic reaction were carried in a cylindrical glassy reactor at constant temperature of 25 °C. An LED source with 50 W was used as a visible source. The suspension containing 0.1 g of the photocatalyst and solution of the pollutant were stirred for 60 min under dark condition to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. At different irradiation times, concentrations of the dyes were analyzed by measuring the absorbances at wavelengths of 553, 477, 664, and 540 nm for RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine dyes, respectively.

The XRD patterns were applied to study crystal structure of the synthesized photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks for pure TiO2 match well with the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystal planes of anatase and the (110), (101), and (111) crystal planes of rutile phases (JCPDS file no. 04- 0477) [35]. For the TiO2/CDs nanocomposites, typical diffraction peaks for CDs cannot be observed, which is attributed to the high dispersion and low content of CDs in the TiO2/CDs nanocomposites [36]. As well as, the XRD patterns of the TiO2/CDs/PANI samples do not show any change compared to that of the pure TiO2. Because, amorphous PANI counterpart has adsorbed onto the TiO2/CDs nanocomposite without affecting its structure [37].

The elemental composition of the fabricated photocatalysts were studied by EDX measurements, as the spectra are exhibited in Fig. 2 . The EDX spectrum for the TiO2 sample contains Ti and O elements. The TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite has composed of Ti, O, and C elements. The peaks of CDs sample are clearly ascribed to C and O elements. For the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites, the presence of Ti, O, C, and N elements are confirmed, suggesting integration of CDs and PANI counterparts with nanoparticles of TiO2. Additionally, elemental mapping of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 2(b–f). It can be clearly seen that Ti, O, C, and N elements have distributed throughout the sample, which confirms distribution of TiO2, CDs, and PANI components of the ternary nanocomposite in whole of the sample. Therefore, it was concluded that CDs and PANI have added to the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. In addition, weight percentages of the elements in the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples were determined and the results are shown in Table 1 . As seen, the experimental and theoretical values are comparable.

The morphology of TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite was studied using SEM and HRTEM instruments and the obtained images are displayed in Fig. 3 . The SEM image of the nanocomposite showed that its spherical particles have highly aggregated (Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b shows the HRTEM image of the ternary photocatalyst. The measured lattice spacings of 0.352 and 0.320 nm are resulted from the (002) plane of CDs and (101) plane of TiO2, respectively.

To explore the surface chemical composition and chemical status of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite, XPS analysis was performed and the spectra are displayed in Fig. 4 . The survey spectrum shows the existence of titanium (Ti 2p), oxygen (O 1s), carbon (C 1s), and nitrogen (N 1s) elements on the surface of this nanocomposite (Fig. 4a). Besides these elements, no other elements were observed, confirming high purity of the ternary nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 4b, two peaks at 458.8 eV (Ti 2p3/2) and 464.5 eV (Ti 2p1/2) are corresponding to the binding energy of Ti4+ ions in the TiO2 nanoparticles [38]. Fig. 4c represents the spectrum for oxygen element (O1s). The peaks at 529.7 and 531.5 eV are ascribed to the presence of TiO and TiOC bonds. The other peak at 533.1 eV is assigned to the Ti−OH group [39]. Meanwhile, the main peaks at 398.5, 399.45, and 400.2 eV are attributed to the benzenoid diamine (NH), the quinoid remain-diimine species (N), and the protonated amine species (–N+) from PANI, respectively (Fig. 4d) [40]. The C1s peak is deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 285.6, 286.5, and 288.8 eV, which are assigned to CC, C–H, C–O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 4e) [41]. Thus, the XPS analysis confirmed the presence of CDs, PANI, and TiO2 counterparts in the structure of the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalyst.

FT-IR spectroscopy is useful technique to analyze the functional groups presented in the structure of photocatalysts. As the spectra displayed in Fig. 5 a, the broad peaks at around 400–600 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching modes of TiO and TiOTi groups, and the absorption peaks in the range of 3340–3550 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of OH group [42,43]. In the FT-IR spectrum of TiO2/CDs(0.5 mL) nanocomposite, the peaks at 1400 and 1600 cm−1 are corresponding to CC bond vibration in aromatic ring [44]. For the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites, the peaks at 1569 and 1485 cm−1 are associated to the stretching vibration of CC (from quinonoid structure) and CC (from benzenoid structure), respectively. In addition, the peaks at 1235 and 3200 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–N and NH bonds, respectively [45,46].

The results for photocatalytic degradation of RhB by the TiO2, TiO2/CDs, and TiO2/CDs/PANI samples under visible light are shown in Fig. 6 a. In each experiment, 0.4 mg/mL of the photocatalysts were added in 250 mL of RhB solution with concentration of 4.8 mg/L. Then the photocatalytic activities of the samples were investigated. As seen, there is almost no degradation of RhB after the light irradiation for 360 min in photolysis condition, indicating that molecules of RhB are sufficiently stable under visible-light irradiation. It is evident that photocatalytic activities of the TiO2/CDs nanocomposites are much higher than that of the TiO2 nanoparticles. Notably, photocatalytic performance of the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites are substantially higher than that of the TiO2 sample. For comparison purpose, the TiO2/PANI (20%) nanocomposite was also applied for photodegradation of RhB under the same conditions. The result obviously displayed that the degradation efficiency of RhB is very lower than the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites. Among the ternary nanocomposites, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalyst displayed the best activity by degrading RhB in 90 min, which is very shorter than the times required for the degradation reaction over the other photocatalysts. Fig. 6(b–d) illustrate variations of RhB spectra during the degradation reactions over the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalysts under visible light. After the light irradiation for 90 min, 99.2% of RhB was photodegraded by the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite, whereas only 61.3% and 21.2% of the dye were degraded by the TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2 photocatalysts, respectively. In Table 2 , photocatalytic activity of the present work was compared with similar reported photocatalytic systems. As seen, either a higher amount of photocatalyst or more reaction time have been used for the degradations of dyes using high power irradiation sources. However in our photocatalytic system, we have used a small amount of photocatalyst for nearly complete degradation of RhB under the light irradiation with the power of 50 W. Therefore, the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite has substantial activity in photocatalytic removal of environmental pollutants.

To get a better comparison between photocatalytic performances of the fabricated photocatalysts, the degradation rate constants over the samples were calculated using pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the results are displayed in Fig. 7 a. The results indicated that the rate constants for photocatalytic removal of RhB over the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples are 13.2 × 10−4, 182 × 10−4, and 481 × 10−4 min−1, respectively. Obviously, the degradation rate constant over the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite is 36.5 and 2.60 times as much as the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively. These results clearly revealed that the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites have clearly improved photocatalytic performances. In order to show separation efficiencies of the photoinduced charge carriers in the photocatalysts, the PL spectra for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples were provided. From Fig. 7b, it can be seen that the PL intensity for the ternary nanocomposite is much lower than those of the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) samples, suggesting more effective separation of the charge carriers in the ternary nanocomposite relative to the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) samples, leading to an enhanced photocatalytic performance. Fig. 7c shows nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms for the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalysts. The hysteresis loops of these photocatalysts can be categorized as type IV, indicating the presence of mesopore structure. Table 3 displays texture parameters of the samples. The specific BET surface area of the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) samples are 45.8, 51.2, and 65.6 m2 g−1, respectively. As a consequence, the greatly enhanced photocatalytic activity of the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite is attributed to improved visible-light absorption, efficiently separation of the charge carriers, and enhanced surface area as confirmed by UV–vis DRS, PL spectroscopy, and BET surface analyses, respectively.

Fig. 8 shows the proposed charge separation mechanism in the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites during the degradation process under visible light. The CB and valence band (VB) potentials of TiO2 are −0.50 and 2.7 eV (vs. NHE). While, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the LUMO of PANI are +0.62 and −2.14 eV (vs. NHE), respectively [32]. As seen, the energy levels of PANI are more negative than those of TiO2. When the TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites are illuminated by visible light, PANI absorbs the light and produces e−/h+ pairs by exciting electrons from its HOMO to LUMO. The photogenerated electrons of PANI move to the CB of TiO2 through CDs, meanwhile the photogenerated holes are effectively collected in the HOMO of PANI [48–51]. Therefore, the photoinduced electrons and holes could be easily and effectively separated and the photoinduced electrons react with O2 molecules to produce O2 − species over the CB of TiO2 (E°(O2/O2
− ) = −0.33 eV/NHE) [52]. However, the photogenerated holes in the HOMO of PANI ( + 0.62 eV/NHE) is less positive to oxidize OH− (1.90 eV/NHE) and H2O (2.70 eV/NHE) to produce OH• radicals. Thus, the photoexcited holes in the HOMO of PANI directly oxidize RhB molecules to produce oxidation products.

The degradation mechanism of RhB by the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite was investigated by performing trapping experiments with adding 2-propanol (2-PrOH), ammonium oxalate (AO), and benzoquinone (BQ) with concentration of 10−3 M into the reaction system for trapping OH, h+, and O2 − species, respectively. Fig. 9 shows that with the addition of these compounds, negative effects on the photocatalytic degradation reaction are observed. Decrease of the degradation rate constant in the presence of BQ is much more than the other compounds. Therefore, the trapping experiments suggested that photogenerated O2 − species, rather than OH and h+ species, are the major active species in the degradation reaction.

To identify the effect of preparation time of the ternary nanocomposite on the photocatalytic performance, additional samples with different preparation times were fabricated. As seen in Fig. 10 a, by increasing preparation time of the photocatalyst from 60 to 360 min, the degradation efficiency increased and reached a plateau at 360 min. To exhibit separation efficiency of the e−/h+ pairs from recombination, PL spectra of the samples were obtained, as shown in Fig. 10b. As observed, the emission intensity of the photocatalyst prepared in 360 min was obviously reduced compared with the nanocomposite prepared in 60 and 240 min. This indicated that recombination rate of the charge carriers in the photocatalyst prepared in 360 min is low, which is beneficial for increasing the photocatalytic performance.

Photodegradation reactions of RhB, MB, and fuchsine (as cationic dyes) and MO (as anionic dye) over the TiO2, TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL), and TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) photocatalysts were investigated and the rate constants are shown in Table 4 . The observed degradation rate constants (kobs ) were evaluated from the experimental data using a linear regression. In all cases, R 2 (correlation coefficient) values are higher than 0.96, which indicate that the first-order kinetic model can well describe the degradation reactions. It is observed that activity of the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite in degradation of fuchsine is nearly 26 and 2.6-folds larger than those of the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively, whereas for MB degradation the enhanced activity is 23 and 1.6-folds, respectively. Additionally, activity of the ternary nanocomposite in degradation of MO is 21.2 and 2.2 times as much as the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively. Thus, the ternary photocatalysts has remarkable activity in degradations of different pollutants, regardless cationic and anionic characteristics.

In order to study the effect of RhB concentration on the photocatalytic degradation under visible-light irradiation, the concentration of RhB was varied in the range of 0.8–1.4 × 10−5 M with the same amount of photocatalyst (0.4 mg/mL) in all experiments. As observed in Fig. 11 , the rate constant decreases with increasing concentration of RhB. At higher concentrations of RhB, more pollutant molecules are adsorbed over the surface of photocatalyst, resulting in occupation of its active sites. Therefore, the reactive species are not extensively produced over the catalyst surface, leading to decrease of the degradation rate constant.

Finally, stability of the ternary TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible light was explored, as the results are displayed in Fig. 12 . As shown, after five cyclic experiments, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite exhibited good photocatalytic performance. As seen, the degradation rate of RhB slowly decreased with time to 84% at the fifth degradation cycle. This decrease was possibly caused by the adsorption of the intermediates of the degradation reaction, leading to deactivation of the photocatalyst. We noticed that the ternary nanocomposite still showed high efficiency even after 5 cycles. Thus, we concluded that photocatalyst shows good photocatalytic stability.

In summary, novel TiO2/CDs/PANI nanocomposites were fabricated by a facile procedure. The TiO2/CDs/PANI photocatalysts exhibited highly enhanced photocatalytic performance toward degradations of different organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation in comparison with the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs photocatalysts. Among the ternary photocatalysts, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite showed the superior activity. Obviously, the rate constant for degradation of RhB over the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposites was 36.5 and 2.60 times as much as the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively. In addition, activity of this ternary nanocomposite in degradation of fuchsine was nearly 26 and 2.6-folds greater than those of the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively, whereas for MB degradation the enhanced activity is 23 and 1.6-folds. Finally, activity of the ternary nanocomposite in MO removal was 21.2 and 2.2 times as much as the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively. Thus, the ternary photocatalysts had remarkable activity in degradations of different pollutants, regardless cationic and anionic characteristics. Finally, the TiO2/CDs/PANI (20%) nanocomposite exhibited good photocatalytic performance without significant deactivation, indicating that the ternary photocatalyst is a kind of stable reusable visible-light-driven photocatalyst.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support ofthis work by University of Mohaghegh Ardabili-Iran is acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, modification, and photo/photoelectrocatalytic degradation applications of TiO2 nanotube arrays: a review

Int. J. Hydrogen Energy
